coredata.dc:title: Tuning visible light-driven photocatalytic NO removal: Insights from glucose-derived CQDs/ZnO nanorods composite
coredata.dc:creator: Tran, Duyen Phuc-Hanh
Pham, Minh-Thuan
Wang, Ya-Fen
Chang, Tien-Chin
You, Sheng-Jie
coredata.dc:description: The potential of visible light photocatalysis in mitigating environmental pollutants has gained significant attention. In this study, we investigate the photocatalytic NO removal efficiency of oxygen defect-rich zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NRs), facilitated by the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) under mild conditions. The successful integration of CQDs with ZnO NRs represents a novel and sustainable approach for efficient NO removal under visible light irradiation. Notably, the CQDs/ZnO NRs shows a substantial increase in NO removal efficiency (71.9%), low toxicity (2.7%) and durability (only 7.7% efficiency reduction after five cycles). The synergetic effects of CQDs and oxygen defects within the ZnO NRs structure, which broadens the light absorption spectrum, facilitates electron separation, thus enhanced photocatalytic NO removal. Furthermore, photoluminescence, active species trapping experiments, and electron spin resonance analysis have confirmed the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 –) during the excitation of electrons. Finally, the synergetic mechanism of CQDs and oxygen defects in ZnO NRs for amplifying visible-light-driven photocatalytic NO removal was elucidated.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The potential of visible light photocatalysis in mitigating environmental pollutants has gained significant attention. In this study, we investigate the photocatalytic NO removal efficiency of oxygen defect-rich zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NRs), facilitated by the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) under mild conditions. The successful integration of CQDs with ZnO NRs represents a novel and sustainable approach for efficient NO removal under visible light irradiation. Notably, the CQDs/ZnO NRs shows a substantial increase in NO removal efficiency (71.9%), low toxicity (2.7%) and durability (only 7.7% efficiency reduction after five cycles). The synergetic effects of CQDs and oxygen defects within the ZnO NRs structure, which broadens the light absorption spectrum, facilitates electron separation, thus enhanced photocatalytic NO removal. Furthermore, photoluminescence, active species trapping experiments, and electron spin resonance analysis have confirmed the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 –) during the excitation of electrons. Finally, the synergetic mechanism of CQDs and oxygen defects in ZnO NRs for amplifying visible-light-driven photocatalytic NO removal was elucidated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing concerns about achieving net-zero and tackling climate change have urged the need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions worldwide. On the other hand, Nitrogen oxide (NOx) emitted from industrial and transportation activities serve as both environmental pollutants and indirect greenhouse gas which play a major role in environmental degradation and climate change phenomena [1,2]. To tackle this issue of NOx pollution, photocatalytic NO removal has drawn considerable attention in recent years due to its potential ability to reduce NOx emissions under environmentally mild conditions [3,4]. Notably, visible-light-driven photocatalysis has gained attraction as it can harness solar energy, rendering it a promising approach for sustainable technological applications. Given the urgency to find an effective solution, it is imperative to choose a photocatalyst that not only exhibits a high efficiency under visible light but also effectively suppresses the formation of harmful intermediates, such as NO2 and N2O4, which could contribute to secondary pollution concerns [5–7].

To date, various photocatalysts have been proven effective for degrading NO in air pollutants, such as TiO2, ZnO, g-C3N4, MgO, CeO2, Bi-based photocatalysts, among others [8–11]. Among these, Zinc oxide (ZnO) has been extensively studied and widely used as a flexible semiconductor material due to its direct band gap (3.37 eV) and high exciton binding energy (60 meV) [12,13]. However, the ZnO nanostructure is plagued by the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and selective sensitivity to UV light, hindering its photocatalytic efficiency [14,15]. Extensive efforts have been made to improve light responsiveness and reduce recombination rates, such as metals and non-metals doping, co-catalysis or tailored defects, etc [4,12,16–18]. It has been demonstrated that the presence of oxygen defects provides an increased number of adsorption and active sites for the photocatalytic reaction, inducing localized energy states within the band gap, thereby enhancing the visible light absorption capacity and removing pollutants [19,20]. In our previous study, oxygen defect-rich ZnO nanorods (ZnO NRs) synthesized by a simple solvothermal method exhibited up to 78.8% NO degradation under solar light irradiation [21]. Although the photocatalytic performance is promising, the oxygen defect-rich ZnO NRs still needs room for further enhancement for the visible-light-driven NO removal efficiency. The rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and limited sensitivity to visible light remain significant challenges hindering the full utilization of ZnO NRs as a highly efficient visible light-driven photocatalyst [22,23].

Recently, glucose-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered considerable attention in the field of photocatalysis due to their outstanding properties such as broad absorption bands, low toxicity, and photo-induced electron transfer characteristics [14,24,25]. In addition, CQDs possess a rich surface chemistry with the various functional groups that enable effective charge transfer, thus suppressing the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [26]. Qin and co-workers indicated that the addition of CQDs extended the visible light absorption range of pure P25 and increased NO removal efficiency by 1.7 times [27]. Similarly, Li and co-workers demonstrated the synergistic effects of oxygen vacancies of TiO2 and CQDs on light absorption performance, band gap structure, electron transfer and separation characteristics during NO removal photocatalysis [28]. However, the research potential in exploring the synergistic effect between CQDs and ZnO NRs in NO removal photocatalysis is still limited.

In this study, we explore the effect of combining CQDs with oxygen defect-rich ZnO NRs on the photocatalytic NO removal under visible light irradiation. Furthermore, the crystal structure, morphology, and optical properties were also investigated to evaluate their effect in enhancing visible light absorption and photocatalytic activity. Finally, the mechanism underlying the enhanced photocatalytic activity is elucidated through trapping experiments and ESR signaling of free radicals (e.g. h+, e–, •OH, •O2 –). Our findings contribute to the fundamental understanding of defect engineering in ZnO NRs-based materials and the potential of glucose-derived carbon quantum dots as a sustainable and efficient solution to mitigate NOx and secondary pollution from photocatalytic NO removal process.

ZnO NRs were synthesized using the optimized one-step solvothermal method from our previous work [21]. In this study, CQDs/ZnO NRs composites were prepared using the same method as for ZnO NRs, with the incorporation of CQDs derived from glucose. An appropriate amount of glucose was added during the ZnO NRs formation, with varying weight ratios (i.e. 3 wt%, 5 wt%, 10 wt%, and 15 wt%) ( Fig. 1). The resulting mixture was sealed in an autoclave and heated at 100 °C for 6 h. After naturally cooled down to room temperature, the precipitates obtained were collected by centrifugation, thoroughly washed with ethanol and deionized water several times, and then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

The crystal structure and phase identification of the as-prepared samples were analyzed through X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å) (Bruker, D8-Advance, Germany) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis (Shimadzu, IR Spirit, Japan). Morphological characteristics were studied using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi FE-SEM S-4800 N, Japan) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEM-2000FXII, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was also conducted to further investigate the surface elemental states and composition (Ulvac-Phi, PHI Hybrid Quantera II, Japan). The light responsiveness and energy band structure of materials were investigated by diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) (Agilent, Cary 5000, US). Photocurrent density-time curve measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist were utilized to investigate the spatial transfer of photo-generated charges and the separation of materials at 1 V vs. NHE, 25 °C and pH 7 of 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte (Autolab PGSTAT101, Metrohm, USA). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface was calculated on micromeritics physisorption (Micromeritics, Tristar 3000, US) with nitrogen adsorption at 77 K. In addition, spin-trapped radicals were identified by UV irradiation of the electron spin resonance (ESR) signal, detected using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trap, DMPO− •OH (water) and DMPO− •O2 − (methanol) (Bruker, EMX-Plus, Germany). In addition, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using an F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 200 nm.

The photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/ZnO NRs was evaluated through NO removal experiments conducted in a continuous flow photoreactor (30 cm × 10 cm x 15 cm) (Fig. S1) [21]. An initial stream containing NO at a concentration of 500 ppb was obtained by diluting air with NO gas (100 ppm) at a controlled flow rate of 1.5 L/min, with a relative humidity (RH) of 70%. In each cycle, 0.2 g of the prepared catalyst was dispersed into 10 ml of DI water on a glass dish (d = 12 cm) using sonication for 10 min, and then dried at 60 °C. When the NO concentration reached an adsorption-desorption equilibrium in the darkness, the prepared samples were illuminated under visible light for 30 min and monitored using a NOx analyzer (Thermo Scientific, Model 42i). The employed visible light source was a 300 W Xenon lamp equipped with a UV cut-off filter to remove UV light below 420 nm. The estimated intensity of the light source was 26.5 mW/cm2. Fig. 2 (a) depicts the XRD patterns of ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs with varying CQDs loading weights. The unique peaks located at 2θ = 31.8°, 34.5°, 36.3°, 47.7°, 56.6°, 62.9°, 66.4°, 67.9°, 69.2°, 72.5°, and 77.2°, corresponding to surfaces (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004), and (202) of hexagonal structure wurtzite ZnO with a (001) direction, respectively (JCPDS 36–1451) [31,32]. In comparison to pure ZnO NRs, the diffraction peaks of CQDs/ZnO become broader and exhibited weaker intensity, implying that the introduction of glucose-derived CQDs into ZnO NRs structure leads to reduce crystallinity and smaller particle size. This result also reveals that the introduction of CQDs affects crystal structure of the CQDs/ZnO NRs, which are attributed to chemical interactions between the CQDs and ZnO NRs [33]. It is attributed to the substitution of O by C and the formation of a Zn–C bond during CQDs/ZnO synthesis process [34,35]. In addition, the presence of carbon atoms in the CQDs/ZnO NRs composite considerably improves electron - hole pair transport and separation rate, thus boosting the photocatalytic NO removal efficiency, as discussed in the following section. Unfortunately, the typical diffraction peaks of CQDs were not detected in the XRD analysis due to the low concentration of CQDs in the CQDs/ZnO NRs composite, a result consistent with previous studies [14,33,36].

To demonstrate the presence of CQDs in the ZnO sample, the FTIR spectra of the ZnO NRs and the CQDs/ZnO NRs samples were analyzed (Fig. 2 (b)). An evident stretching peak was observed around 3450 cm–1, indicating the presence of adsorbed water on the surface of synthesized ZnO. This water-induced vibration also promoted O–H stretching on the ZnO. In addition, the peaks identified within the wavenumber range of 490–510 cm–1 correspond to the stretching and bending vibrations of Zn–O bonds, consistent with those observed in synthesized ZnO [21]. In the CQDs/ZnO NRs composite, the characteristic peaks located at 1640 cm−1, 1455 cm−1 and 1081 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of C = O, C H and C OH, respectively [25,33,36]. Thus, it can be inferred that CQDs are successfully decorated on the surface of ZnO NRs. In addition, the characteristic peaks in the FTIR spectrum of CQDs/ZnO NRs composites tend to shift to higher wavenumber states. This indicates that the addition of CQDs induces surface interaction with ZnO NRs, thus confirming the successful incorporation of CQDs into this material. The findings from FTIR analysis align with the XRD analysis.

SEM and EDS mapping analysis were employed to investigate the morphological characteristics of synthesized ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO-NRs ( Fig. 3). Generally, the pristine ZnO NRs exhibit the largest size among all the samples (Fig. 3 (a)). In the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs, the rod-like morphology still persisted but with a smaller rod length and rice-like CQDs particles. As depicted in Fig. 3 (b,c), the SEM images of the CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibit a reduction in rod size (32–36 nm) compared with the pure ZnO (44–50 nm), resulting from self-assembled agglomeration [33]. This is consistent with the results of the XRD analysis discussed above and previous studies [37,38]. In addition, the SEM-EDS mapping was conducted to assess the elements present in CQDs/ZnO NRs, revealing the presence of Zn, O, and C (Fig. 3 (d-g)). As a result, only Zn, O and C were present in the EDS analysis of the CQDs/ZnO NRs composite with the atomic percentages of 34.98%, 34.87% and 30.15%, respectively. In addition, the crystal structure of small CQDs was observed more clearly in HR-TEM with the smaller-sized CQDs coating the ZnO NRs ( Fig. 4). It is noteworthy that the presence of CQDs exhibits a strong interaction with the original structure of ZnO NRs ( Fig. 4 (b,c,d)). This promotes charge transfer and enhances the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnO NRs composites. This is completely consistent with previous XRD and FTIR analysis.

Meanwhile, the surface properties of the materials were evaluated through the N2 absorption - desorption isotherms, as illustrated in Fig. 5 and Table 1. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area for pristine ZnO NRs was determined to be 26.24 m²/g, whereas the composite material, 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs, exhibited a slightly reduced value of 25.46 m²/g. Notably, the incorporation of CQDs into the ZnO NRs structure led to a minor reduction in the active surface area. This phenomenon can be attributed to the agglomeration of the ZnO NRs during the synthesis, hindering N2 absorption on the surface of the carbon atoms, thereby diminishing the surface contribution of the carbon component in the composite [39]. In addition, the pore volume and diameter of CQDs/ZnO NRs were reduced compared to pristine ZnO NRs. Specifically, the ZnO NRs displayed an average adsorption pore diameter of 23.25 nm, while the composite material registered an adsorption pore diameter of 21.59 nm. This data signifies a statistically significant reduction in pore diameter upon the inclusion of CQDs [40]. Moreover, the pore volume decreased to approximately 1.2 times its original value, 0.195 cm³ /g and 0.179 cm³ /g for pristine ZnO NRs and 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs respectively. This suggests that the introduced CQDs altered the pore structure properties, in agreement with the SEM and HR-TEM analysis discussed above. However, specific surface area may not the sole factor influencing photocatalytic activity [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and band structures of as-prepared materials
ce:section.ce:para: X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to assess the surface chemical states of ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs. Fig. 6 (a) illustrates the XPS survey spectra of ZnO NRs and 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs. The results revealed the presence of zinc, oxygen, and carbon, indicating a high degree of purity in the synthesized ZnO NRs and 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs. Furthermore, the spectral peaks were also found to correlate with the Zn (2p), O (1 s), and C (1 s), which is shown in Fig. 6 (b-d). The presence of carbon is attributed to surface carbon absorption from ambient air exposure. The charge displacement is thus compensated for all binding energies using the C (1 s) peak of the graphite carbon (284.6 eV) as a reference.

Fig. 6 (b) provides detailed spectra of Zn (2p), revealing two symmetric peaks at binding energies of 1022 and 1045 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively. In comparison with pristine ZnO NRs, the Zn (2p) binding energy shifts towards higher values in the direction of CQDs/ZnO NRs, implying that carbon can improve the electron attraction ability of Zn2+ [42,43]. This finding agreed with a previous study, highlighting that the incorporations of CQDs may affect Zn2+ via chelation, and the oxygen-active sites in ZnO would be occupied by carboxyl compounds afterward [33,44]. As a result, electron migration boosts the migration rate of free electrons, thereby increasing the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnO NRs.

The O (1 s) spectra of ZnO NRs exhibit a distinct peak at 530.9 eV, as depicted in Fig. 6 (c), this peak is attributed to the lattice oxygen (O2 −) ions in Zn–O bonding of wurtzite ZnO [45]. In addition to the main lattice oxygen peak at 530.9 eV, two additional shoulder peaks emerged at 532.3 eV and 534.2 eV. The peak at 532.3 eV is associated with OH− group adsorbed on the surface of ZnO NRs [35,37,46]. The identified peak at 534.2 eV can be linked to CO bonding that was caused by the presence of adsorbed oxygen species on the sample surface [37]. Obviously, a slight peak shift in the CQDs/ZnO NRs structure implies the formation of CO induced by carbon addition, significantly influencing the binding energy of the composite [44]. In addition, the relatively higher content of CO bonding from adsorbed oxygen species in the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs compared to the ZnO NRs, demonstrating that the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs have superior adsorption properties.

The C (1 s) spectrum can be identified into two peaks centered at 285.5 and 286.7 eV, corresponding to ZnO NRs and 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs, respectively, attributed to C–C bonding with sp2 orbital (Fig. 6 (d)) [47]. The additional peak at 286.7 eV corresponds to oxy-genated carbons (the CO bond with alcoholic carbon, the CO bonding with carbonyl carbon, or the OCO bond with carboxyl carbon), revealing surface functional groups on the CQDs [28,48]. Furthermore, with CQDs addition, the peak intensities of C (1 s) decrease obviously compared to ZnO NRs. This could result from the partial carbonization of glucose monomers in the presence of some residual oxygen functional groups [49]. This aligns with the increased oxygen intensity in the C–doped sample and concurs with previous work, indicating that excess oxygen favors C–O bond formation rather than metalC formation [50].

Fig. 7 depicts the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) used to characterize the optical absorption of the samples. Apparently, the introduction of carbon changed the absorption edge of 10% CQDs/ZnO to 410 nm, while the absorption edge of pristine ZnO NRs remained at around 398 nm (Fig. 7 (a)). It can be observed that the change in absorbance in the visible light range is related to the color change from white to dark yellow during the sample’s synthesis process. Simultaneously, electrostatic interactions between the carbon and zinc oxide also contribute to this shift [39,51]. In addition, the fluorescence behavior of CQDs aqueous solution was characterized under daylight and UV light conditions (Fig. S2). It is clearly showed that the green emission of CQDs is attributed to the band gap change of CQDs, which occurs due to the integration of oxygen moieties on the surface structures [52]. This phenomenon is intrinsically linked to the nano size and composition of CQDs. Eventually, enhanced photon adsorption results in improving the photocatalytic efficiency. This finding indicates that the binary composite synthesis enhances visible-light absorption. In contrast, the band gap energy does not change significantly, which are 3.16 and 3.15 eV for ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs, respectively. This is attributed to ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs existing as two distinct phases, so their bandgap energies are not significantly affected when combined [44].

The separation and electromigration properties of ZnO NRs and 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs were then determined through photo transient current measurements, current intensity–time (I−t) curves for the photoelectrodes with the on-off cycles of continuous light irradiation ( Fig. 7 (c)). Photocurrent responses of ZnO NRs and 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs were observed through periodic on/off cycling of visible light irradiation in a 1 M Na2SO4 solution. While ZnO NRs have many advantages in photocatalysis, their photocurrent generation and photocatalytic performance are impeded by the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers [13]. In contrast, the photocurrent of the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs is higher than that of the ZnO NRs. The result suggests that the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs sample exhibits superior charge transportation capabilities with support from CQDs, which leads to enhance the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized material. Consistent with DRS results, the absorption wavelength of the prepared materials under visible light indicates that the light absorption capacity of the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs exceeds that of the ZnO NRs under both the UV and visible light ranges, corresponding to the signal observed when toggling the visible light source on and off. On the electrolyte/semiconductor interface, electrons are transported to the back contact, while holes are trapped or captured by reducing agents in the electrolyte [53]. Furthermore, when the light is turned off, the steady state achieved by both materials is approximately similar. These results imply that CQDs might not play a significant role in mitigating charge carrier recombination in 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs.

Nyquist plots over the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 1 kHz with potential at 1 V versus NHE were utilized to understand the charge migration and transfer behaviors in prepared materials. The ElS Nyquist plots can show a well-defined semicircular shape, which is related to the resistance of the junction interface. According to the results in Fig. 7 (d), the charge transfer efficiency of 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs is better than that of ZnO NRs due to the semicircular morphology of 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs is smaller than that of ZnO NRs. This result indicates higher spatial separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers in 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs. Considering the results of the photocurrent density-time curve, it is evident that the spatial charge separation efficiency of 10% CQDs/ZnO NR has been significantly enhanced with the support of CQDs.

To evaluate the photocatalytic NO removal effficiency, various photocatalytic running tests were conducted under visible light irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 8 (a), it was found that the presence of carrbon in CQDs/ZnO NRs substantially enhanced photocatalytic NO removal compared to pristine ZnO NRs. Notably, the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibit the highest photocatalytic performance, increasing by 9.7% compared to ZnO NRs under visible light irradiation. However, as the CQDs loading reaches 15 wt%, the NO photocatalytic removal tends to decrease. This phenomenon could be attributed to excessive CQDs adhering to the surface of ZnO, which could hampers the light absorption ability and weakens the interaction between the photocatalyst and contaminant [36,54].

The NO removal efficiency and the NO2 conversion yield of the materials are depicted in Fig. 8 (b). These results have shown that both ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibit a low NO2 conversion yield of less than 10%. Interestingly, the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs possess the lowest NO2 conversion efficiency at 2.7%, which is greatly lower than of pure ZnO NRs. This result is comparably higher than other photocatalytic materials such as ZnO/TiO2 (7.18%) [55], TiO2/g-C3N4 (5.3%) [56], etc ( Table 2). The CQDs/ZnO NRs demonstrate the highest photocatalytic NO removal efficiency and the lowest NO2 production. In addition, the estimated apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) and DeNOx index of the photocatalysts are depicted in Fig. 8 (c). The DeNOx index value was significantly improved from 239.3 to 351.6 for 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs under visible light. The maximum photon efficiency was found to be 12.2 × 10−4%, about 1.1 times larger than that of ZnO NRs, and decreased to 11.2 × 10−4% as CQDs concentration exceeded 15 wt%. The significant increase in reaction rate and AQE can be attributed to the efficient separation process of electron-hole pair on CQDs/ZnO NRs. Thus, it is revealed that CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibit favorable attributes for practical conditions, given their high NO removal performance, low toxicity and efficient charge separation.

In this study, 0.2 g of 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs was subjected to five times repeated photocatalytic NO degradation tests in order to assess its reusability and stability ( Fig. 8 (d)). The NO removal efficiency was 70.9%, 67%, 64.2%, 64.7%, and 63.2% under visible light. The removal efficiency of NO declined by 7.7% after 5 cycles experiment, demonstrating that the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs maintains greater reusability and stability under visible light. There are two underlining reasons attributed to the slight decrease in photocatalytic efficiency of 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs. The first reason is due to the unavoidable loss from the material recovery process. The latter cause is probably because of NO2 – and NO3 – as an intermediates accumulate at each cycle of photocatalytic reactions [3]. The presence of NO3 − was confirmed by FTIR analysis both before and after irradiation (Fig. 8 (e)). In addition to that, peaks were identified at 1055 and 1386 cm–1 which possibly represent the stretching phase of NO3 − compound groups [1,57]. Furthermore, the FTIR and XRD analysis for CQDs/ZnO NRs before and after recycling test demonstrates the synthesized composites are very durable and could sustain through numerous photocatalytic cycles (Fig. 8 (e-f)). This result further confirms the stability of CQDs/ZnO NRs under visible light irradiation and highlights a high potential for practical applications, offering elevated NO degradation efficiency and minimal toxic NO2 conversion yield.

In summary, the CQDs/ZnO NRs composite offers significant advantages, such as enhanced photocatalytic efficiency, improved charge separation, and sustained activity, making it a promising candidate for NO removal applications. However, the optimal CQDs loading concentration and the reduction in active surface area should be considered to maximize performance.

Photoluminescence (PL) analysis was employed to investigate the optical properties of ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs. The PL spectra for both ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibited distinctive emission peaks. Firstly, a prominent ultraviolet emission peak was observed around 400 nm, originating from the recombination of free electrons within the near-band-edge emission of ZnO NRs [58]. In addition, a broad visible emission band was observed, ranging from 500 nm to 550 nm, which is commonly attributed to surface-related defects [59,60]. Obviously, the addition of CQDs into the ZnO NRs structure, the PL intensity is lower than that of the pure ZnO NRs, suggesting that the CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibit a reduced recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers, thus leading to a higher photocatalytic efficiency. Hence, it reveals that the synergistic effect of CQDs and oxygen defects in the ZnO NRs structure, effectively enhances the excitation-separation of electrons which is beneficial for improving the light absorption properties of the photocatalyst [59,61,62]. The findings are consistent with the EIS analyses, underscoring the superior efficacy of CQDs/ZnO NRs in NO removal.

Trapping experiments were conducted to elucidate the photocatalytic NO removal mechanism and assess the active radical species under visible light irradiation condition. Various scavengers compounds such as isopropanol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), potassium iodide (KI), and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were employed to capture hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (•O2 –), holes (h+), and electrons (e–), respectively [21]. As depicted in Fig. 9 (b), the photocatalytic NO removal over the 10% CQDs/ZnO demonstrated a significant change attributed to the use of KI as a scavenger. Specifically, KI substantially hindered NO degradation, highlighting the crucial role of holes (h+) in the degradation process. Moreover, the presence of K2Cr2O7 as a representative for e
ion also play a role to the total NO removal, reducing 1.8 times compared to the 10% CQDs/ZnO original efficiency under visible light irradiation. Conversely, the addition of IPA and p-BQ scavengers led to a light decrease in the photocatalytic NO removal of 12.7% and 15.4% under visible light, respectively. Clearly, trapping test results indicate that both electrons and holes contribute equally to the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/ZnO NRs. Furthermore, based on reaction rates, the rates for the first 10 min under visible light for samples trapped by KI and K2Cr2O7 were similar. However, after this period, the reaction rate of the sample trapped by KI tended to decrease gradually until the end of the reaction, leading to a lower rate of •O2 – production compared to •OH.

To further confirm the presence of reactive oxygen species, ESR analysis was employed under visible light irradiation (> 400 nm) with a duration of 300 s, including aqueous-state hydroxl radical (•OH) and superoxide anion radical (•O2 –) in methanol solutions. As depicted in Fig. 9 (c), no signals were detected for •OH and •O2 – under dark condition on CQDs/ZnO NRs. Furthermore, ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs have shown clear signals for •OH and •O2 –, indicating the formation of these radicals during photo-excitation process. In addition, the signal for •O2 – were slightly weaker compared to those for •OH, highlighting an interesting preference for •OH production in both ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs during photo-excitation process. These results are consistent with experimental findings from trapping tests. Although electrons and holes play equally important roles in photocatalytic activity, the •OH radical primarily contributes to the degradation of NO through reduction processes.

Based on the previous findings, the photocatalytic mechanism of the CQDs/ZnO NRs for photocatalytic NO removal can be depicted ( Fig. 9 (d)). Under the stimulation of visible light, electrons (e–) from the valence band (VB) of the material are excited and move up to the conduction band (CB), creating an excess e– in the CB and a hole (h+) in the VB (Eq. 5). These e– can directly move outside to reduce O2, forming •O2 –. These •O2 – will then decompose NO and produce NO2 and other by-products through reduction reactions. On the other hand, the e– also move through CQDs and continue to react with O2 to produce •O2 – for the NO reduction reaction. Meanwhile, the incorporation of CQDs into ZnO NRs act as light harvesters and converters, enhancing the light utilization efficiency of the material [33]. In addition, the transfer of e– in this manner contributes to reducing the recombination between e– h+ pairs, which in turn increases the lifetime of the e– and enhances the overall photocatalytic efficiency [36,63]. At the same time, the h+ in the material's valence band (VB) react with H2O to form •OH. These •OH react with NO to produce NO2 and other by-products through oxidation [63] (Eq. 8). Consequently, both •O2 – and •OH exhibit robust redox activity, reacting with NO and NO2 and forming less toxic NO2 -, NO3 – that aggregating on the surface of photocatalyst [64] (Eqs. 9–11). The following reactions occur during this process:
(5)
ZnO + hv → h
VB / ZnO
+
+
e
CB / ZnO
–
(6)
e
CB / ZnO + C → ZnO +
e
CB / CQD
–
–
(7)

→ C + •

(8)

(9) NO + •O2 – → NO3
(10) NO + •OH → HNO2
(11) HNO2 + •OH → NO3 –

In summary, we have successfully incorporated glucose-derived carbon quantum dots into the structure of ZnO NRs. The experimental results revealed that the 10% CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibited the highest NO removal photocatalytic efficiency, increasing by 9.7% compared with pure ZnO NRs. Furthermore, the CQDs/ZnO NRs demonstrated a significantly reduced NO2 conversion efficiency NO2 (2.7%) and the formation of less toxic NO3 – ions on the photocatalyst surface, making it a favorable candidate for NO removal while minimizing the formation of toxic by-products. Notably, the CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibits excellent stability and reusability, maintaining high photocatalytic efficiency even after five degradation cycles. Finally, based on the above findings, the proposed photocatalysis mechanism describes a sequential process involving charge separation, light harvesting, and redox reactions facilitated by the synergistic effects of CQDs and rich-oxygen defects ZnO NRs. Future research efforts may explore additional optimization strategies, such as surface modification and engineering, to further enhance the performance of CQDs/ZnO NRs composites and expand its applicability in real-world environmental remediation scenarios.
